coredata.dc:title: Electric field polarized sulfonated carbon dots/NiFe layerd double hydroxide as highly efficient electrocatalyst for oxygen evolution reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Wenxiang
Chen, Siyu
Liao, Fan
Zhao, Xiaodong
Shi, Huixian
Shi, Yandi
Xu, Lai
Shao, Qi
Kang, Zhenhui
Shao, Mingwang
coredata.dc:description: Interfacial design and regulation for electrocatalysts can effectively enhance the performance of oxygen evolution reaction (OER). It is still a challenge to search for a simple and easy way to controllably adjust the interface of catalysts. Here we report a new strategy, electric field polarization to regulate the charged functional groups of nickel–iron layered double hydroxide (NiFe LDH)/carbon dots (CDs) composite catalysts. The selectively exposed sulfonate functional groups on CDs under the electric field have regulated the interfacial environment of the composite catalyst. The density function theory (DFT) calculation also reveals that the high electrocatalytic activity can be attributed to H+ capture from *OH or *OOH species by the sulfonated groups on the surface of CDs. Thus, the synergistic effect between the catalytic activity of NiFe LDH and the functional group-modified CDs enhances the OER performance of the composite catalyst, showing low overpotential of 200 mV at 10 mA cm−2 and outstanding kinetics with Tafel slope of 55.2 mV dec−1. Moreover, the polarized composite catalysts can reach a high current density of 51.7 mA cm−2 at 1.46 V vs. reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE), which is 9.2 times higher than the unpolarized one (5.6 mA cm−2).
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
NiFe LDH
Oxygen evolution reaction
Electrocatalysis
Electric field polarization

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of sulfonated CDs (SO3-CDs) and SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work is supported by National MCF Energy R&D Program (2018YFE0306105), National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFA0204800, 2020YFA0406104, 2020YFA0406103, 2020YFA0406101), Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51821002), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51902217, 91961120, 51725204, 21771132, 51972216, 52041202‬), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190041), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), and the 111 Project and Joint International Research Laboratory of Carbon-Based Functional Materials and Devices .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of working electrode fabrication process.

Characterization of NiFe LDH composites. (a) XRD patterns of NiFe LDH (pink), P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH (blue) and SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH (olive green). (b) and (c) TEM images and (d) HRTEM image of SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH. (e) TEM image of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH and (f) HRTEM image of a single P-SO3-CDs particle. (g) HAADF-STEM image and corresponding EDS mapping with C (blue), S (red), Ni (orange), and Fe (yellow) of SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH.

Electrochemical performance of NiFe LDH-based OER catalysts: (a) iR-corrected polarization curves of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH, SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH, NiFe LDH and commercial RuO2 catalysts in 1 M KOH. (b) overpotential (j = 10 mA cm−2) of the prepared OER catalysts. (c) Tafel plots of the prepared OER catalysts. and (d) Chronopotentiometry curves of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH and SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH at 10 mA cm−2, respectively.

(a) XPS survey spectra. (b) high-resolution S 2p spectra of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH and SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH, the inset being the S/C molar ratio of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH and SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH. Raman (c) and FT-IR (d) spectra of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH, SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH, NiFe LDH and SO3-CDs, respectively.

DFT simulation and the schematic illustrating of OER mechanism. (a) DFT simulation of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH and NiFe LDH for OER. Stepwise OER Gibbs free energy of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH and NiFe LDH catalysts. (b) Schematic diagram for OER reaction mechanism with P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH in alkaline solution.

head.ce:title: Electric field polarized sulfonated carbon dots/NiFe layerd double hydroxide as highly efficient electrocatalyst for oxygen evolution reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM), Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices, Soochow University, 199 Ren'ai Road, Suzhou 215123, Jiangsu, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Functional Nano & Soft Materials (FUNSOM)
Jiangsu Key Laboratory for Carbon-Based Functional Materials & Devices

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: W. Zhu and S. Chen contributed equally.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH was synthesized through electrochemical and hydrothermal methods. The P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH catalyst work electrode was obtained via polarized by an electric field of 10,000 V/cm for 30 mins. After that, sulfonate functional groups selectively enrich at the surface of catalysts. Such sulfonate functional groups have a strong ability to capture proton, exhibit excellent OER performances as confirmed by DFT calculation and experimental results.

Interfacial design and regulation for electrocatalysts can effectively enhance the performance of oxygen evolution reaction (OER). It is still a challenge to search for a simple and easy way to controllably adjust the interface of catalysts. Here we report a new strategy, electric field polarization to regulate the charged functional groups of nickel–iron layered double hydroxide (NiFe LDH)/carbon dots (CDs) composite catalysts. The selectively exposed sulfonate functional groups on CDs under the electric field have regulated the interfacial environment of the composite catalyst. The density function theory (DFT) calculation also reveals that the high electrocatalytic activity can be attributed to H+ capture from *OH or *OOH species by the sulfonated groups on the surface of CDs. Thus, the synergistic effect between the catalytic activity of NiFe LDH and the functional group-modified CDs enhances the OER performance of the composite catalyst, showing low overpotential of 200 mV at 10 mA cm−2 and outstanding kinetics with Tafel slope of 55.2 mV dec−1. Moreover, the polarized composite catalysts can reach a high current density of 51.7 mA cm−2 at 1.46 V vs. reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE), which is 9.2 times higher than the unpolarized one (5.6 mA cm−2).

ce:section.ce:para: With the vigorous development of human society, the accompanying energy and environmental crisis becomes even serious, which stimulates the research interest in the conversion and storage of alternative and sustainable energy [1–6]. Electrocatalytic water splitting is the most effective method to produce and store hydrogen energy in chemical form. Oxygen evolution reaction (OER), as an important half reaction in water decomposition, has received a lot of research in recent years [7–8].

It is disappointing that OER is a complex four-electron process with kinetically sluggish and limited the rate of water splitting [7–10]. Therefore, efficient electrocatalysts are needed to reduce overpotential in OER reactions, thus accelerating reactions and improving energy conversion efficiency. Currently, metal oxides such as IrO2 and RuO2 are generally regarded as the most active and durable catalysts in acidic and alkaline media [8–9]. However, it is unrealistic and uneconomical to widely use these precious metal oxide catalysts, due to their scarcity and high cost. On this basis, extensive research has been focused on first-row transition-metal-based oxides materials [11–14] for their high activity, durability and low-cost. In addition, regulating the surface interface structure and composition of nano-electrocatalysts can effectively improve their electrocatalytic activity arouse people's attention [15–16].

Layered double hydroxides (LDHs), also known as hydrotalc-like clays, are regarded as a very promising alternative to noble metal-based OER catalysts due to their flexible structure and wide range of chemical versatility [17–19]. Among them, nickel–iron layered double hydroxide (NiFe LDH) catalysts attract the extensive attention for their low cost and excellent electrocatalytic properties [20–23]. However, their poor electrical conductivity has hindered their further practical applications [19]. Recently, enormous efforts have been applied to improve the electrocatalytic activity of LDH-based catalysts, such as construction of composite with carbon-related materials (carbon nanotubes, graphene and carbon dots) [17–19,22]. fabrication of a single layer structure through exfoliating [22–25]. surface modification of precious metals [26], sulfurization [27], interlamellar anion exchange to alter electronic structures [28], manufacturing defects to increase active sites [29] and doping [30].

Carbon dots (CDs) is a novel kind of carbon nanomaterial, and the CDs family usually includes very small carbon nanoparticles, graphene quantum dots, graphene carbon quantum dots, amorphous CDs and polymer quantum dots. By and large, they can be defined as a spherical carbon-based material (and/or graphite shred) with a size less than 10 nm. CDs were first found in the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes prepared by arc discharge [31]. Since then, this new carbon nanomaterial was widely used in photocatalysis and electrocatalysis [32–34]. The surface of CDs rich in functional groups. It has been reported that modification of functional groups on the surface of catalysts is a highly effective method to enhance catalytic activity [35–36]. CDs may play a key role in enhancing OER of NiFe LDH composites [22]. Meanwhile, the sulfonate functional groups on CDs can capture H+ from *OH or *OOH during OER catalysis, thus significantly reducing OER overpotential [36]. It is obvious that only a few of the sulfonate functional groups may be exposed on the surface of the catalysts. In order to make full use of these groups, we introduce an electric field to selectively polarize and adjust the interfacial environment of the composite catalyst via revealing more sulfonate functional groups on the surface of catalysts, which may profit the electrocatalytic reaction. Electrical-field polarization causes the enrichment of the sulfonated group on the surface of the catalyst. The LDH in sulfonated group dominant environment leads to the change of the electronic structure of hydrotalcite and thus the adsorption of the reaction intermediate, which favors the catalysis of OER.

Therefore, we first synthesized sulfonated CDs/NiFe layered double hydroxide (SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH) composites via a simple hydrothermal method. Then an electric field is applied to polarize the catalyst during the fabrication of the working electrode to selectively expose charged functional groups. Experimental results show that the polarized-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH (P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH) could be used as a highly efficient OER electrocatalyst with a low overpotential of 200 mV at the current density of 10 mA cm−2, a small Tafel slope of 55.2 mV dec-1 and perfect electrochemical stability over 25 h. Density functional theory (DFT) calculations were conducted, which indicates that the enhanced OER electrocatalytic activity is originated from the remarkable decrease of the reaction barrier from *OH to *O and *OOH to *O2 in OER.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of sulfonated CDs (SO3-CDs) and SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH
ce:section.ce:para: SO3-CDs were synthesized by electrochemical etching methods that have been reported [22,37]. SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH was synthesized with a typical hydrothermal method. In brief, 5 mg SO3-CDs were dispersed in redistilled water (10 mL) with an ultrasound method. Next, 0.4 mL of 1 M ferric trichloride (FeCl3) aqueous solution, 1.2 mL of 1 M nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O) aqueous solution and 5 mL of SO3-CDs dispersion were mixed in the beaker containing 52.4 mL water. Then 14 mL of urea aqueous solution (0.2 M) and 2 mL of trisodium citrate (0.01 M) were added into the mixture, respectively. The mixture was stirred for 4 h at 85 °C, and transferred to a 100 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave, which was heated to 120 °C and kept this temperature for 12 h. The SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH was collected by centrifugation, washed with water, and dried at 40 °C in vacuum. The NiFe LDH was prepared with the similar way without the use of CDs.

Vienna Ab Initio Simulation Package (VASP) software was used for this calculation of OER of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH and NiFe LDH [38–39]. In total energy calculations, we set PBE (Perdew–Burke–Ernzerhof)–GGA (generalized gradient approximation) to determine exchange–correlation energies, and set potentials with a plane wave cut-off energy (PAW) to 450 eV. The convergence threshold for energy was set as 10-5 eV, and 0.05 eV·A-1 was chosen as threshold for the interatomic forces [40–43]. The K-points were set as 1 × 2 × 1 for all calculations. To describe the van der Waals interaction, we used the DFT-D3 correction in the Grimme scheme [44]. The difference of free energy in OER is given by the following formula: ΔG = ΔE + ΔEZPE -TΔS, and ΔEZPE is the difference in zero point energy in each step and ΔE is the difference in energy in each step. T is temperature and the temperature parameter of each calculation step was set to 298.15 K. The energy of *O, *OH, and *OOH is calculated based on the above parameters, the * represents P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH or NiFe LDH. Then we got the reaction coordinate based on the energy of the intermediate state.

Fig. 1 illustrates the fabrication of polarized-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH (P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH) catalyst work electrode. In our experiment, the SO3-CDs were synthesized by a one-step electrochemical method [45]. And the SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH was synthesized directly through a hydrothermal method. The SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH suspension was dropped on GCE. After that, the catalyst thin film was polarized by an electric field of 10000 V·cm−1 for 30 min, then the P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH catalyst work electrode was obtained. The schematic diagram of electric field polarization is shown in Figure S1.

Fig. 2 a shows the X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patterns of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH, SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH and NiFe LDH. The strong diffraction peaks of NiFe LDH may be indexed as α-Ni(OH)2 standard card (JCPDS: 38–0715), which is consistence with previous work as reported by Kang et.al [22]. And there is no other characteristic peak from these patterns. Due to small carbon content in the composite, the characteristic peak for carbon at 2θ value of 26° is too weak to be observed. The XRD pattern of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH show there is no significant difference compared with SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH.

The morphology, size, and structure of the SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH were further characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) as shown in Fig. 2b to 2d, revealing that the width of the nanosheets was 50 ~ 200 nm. It is almost transparent under the electron beam due to their ultrathin nature. Atomic force microscopic (AFM) image further confirmed the thickness of nanosheets was estimated to be 3~ 4 nm (Figure S2). The TEM images of NiFe LDH were shown in Figure S3.

To further understand the structure of the SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH, we analyze the crystal structure of the particles in the red frame region shown in Fig. 2c. The high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image (Fig. 2d) reveals the spacing of 0.322 nm corresponds to the (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon, while the intersection angles between NiFe LDH (100) and NiFe LDH (010) are 60°, which strong corroborated the formation of SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH composites. Fig. 2e and 2f reveals that SO3-CDs (size of ~ 5 nm) and the NiFe LDH nanosheet were successfully formed a composite, with SO3-CDs evenly distributed on the NiFe LDH nanosheet. The high-angle annular dark-field scanning TEM (HAADF-STEM) image of SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH are shown in Fig. 2e, with the corresponding elemental mapping showing the element distribution of with C (blue), S (red), Ni (orange), and Fe (yellow). As shown in Figure S4, the Ni/Fe atomic ratio of the SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH has little change before and after the polarization which measured by EDS. And the BET surface area (Figure S5) of the SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH is almost unchanged before (145.8 cm3 g−1) and after (146.9 cm3 g−1) polarization.

The as-prepared NiFe LDH-based composites were investigated as the OER electrocatalyst in a typical three-electrode system in 1 M KOH with a scan rate of 5 mV·s−1. To remove the generated oxygen bubbles, the working electrode rotates continuously at a speed of 1600 rotations per minute (rpm) during the measurement. For comparison, three reference samples were also tested under the same test conditions. Fig. 3 a exhibits the iR-corrected linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves of OER for the prepared catalysts, and uncorrected polarization curves were shown in Figure S6. A significant oxidation peak can be seen on the iR-corrected LSV curve, which indicates the Ni2+/Ni3+ redox reaction in NiFe LDH nanosheets. As shown in Fig. 3a and 3b, P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH exhibits a good OER performance with the overpotential of 200 mV at the current density of 10 mA cm−2, which is 46, 91 and 110 mV smaller than SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH (246 mV), NiFe LDH (291 mV) and the commercial RuO2 (310 mV), respectively. Moreover, Fig. 3a shows that P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH can reach a high current density of 51.7 mA cm−2 at 1.46 V vs. reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE), which is 9.2 times higher than the SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH (5.6 mA cm−2). The LSV curves of the pure SO3-CDs shown in Figure S7 indicated its negligible OER performance. The electrochemical impedance spectrum (EIS) was shown in Figure S8. The Nyquist of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH, SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH, NiFe LDH and commercial RuO2 were carried out in the frequency ranging from 100 kHz to 0.01 Hz in 1 M KOH solution at potential in the range of 1.4 V (vs. RHE), indicating that the P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH has the best conductivity among these four catalysts.

Tafel slope is also an important factor for evaluating the OER dynamics, which describes the effect of the potential on the current density. The Tafel data is obtained on the basis of LSV curves in Fig. 3a. As shown in Fig. 3c, P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH exhibits the fastest kinetics with a Tafel slope of 55.2 mV dec-1, which are lower than SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH (58.5 mV dec-1), NiFe LDH (80.3 mV dec-1) and the commercial RuO2 (86.7 mV dec-1), respectively. The electrochemical double-layer capacitance (Cdl) was show in Figure S9, and the electrochemically active surface areas (ECSA) also can be roughly estimated from Figure S9. Table S1 shows the Cdl and the ECSA of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH were 13.14 mF cm−2 and 46.45 cm−2, which are larger than any other catalysts. In addition, the faraday efficiency of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH electrode was calculated to 97.5% (Figure S10).

Moreover, P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH not only had the highest activity among the catalysts we measured, but also performed good electrochemical stability. Fig. 3d shows that the durability of prepared catalysts was evaluated at the current density of 10 mA cm−1. After a 25 h test, P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH still maintains the original performance of 98.2%, significantly better than those of SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH (90% of the original performance can be maintained within only 20 h) and commercial RuO2 (maintains the original performance of 93% within only 15 h, Figure S11). After durability test, the layered structure of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH still unchanged as shown in Figure S12. The good stability of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH may be attributed to the enhanced interaction between SO3-CDs and NiFe LDH under the action of the electric field polarization. The OER performance of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH was also compared with the other catalysts from recently published works as listed in Table S2. The P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH exhibits excellent OER performance.

The enhanced OER performance of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH may be originated from the electric field polarization. Figs. 4 and S13 show the typical survey scan X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH, SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH and NiFe LDH, which corroborated the existence of both C, O, S, Fe and Ni in the as-synthesized composites. Both of Fig. 4 a and 4b clearly show that the surfaces of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH exposes more S content than that of SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH, with the S/C molar ratio of the former up to 3.4 times as large as that of the latter. Figure S13a shows that the high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH and SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH, with three peaks located at 284.6 eV, 285.6 eV, 288.2 eV corresponding to the C–C, C-O, C = O. As shown in Figure S13c, the high-resolution Fe 2p XPS spectrum reveals that most of the Fe species are in the + 3 oxidation state.

In the subsequent experiments, we further characterized SO3-CDs, NiFe LDH and the P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH composites. The Raman spectra (Fig. 4c) of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH (blue), SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH (green), and SO3-CDs (black) all show two characteristic peaks located at 1326 and 1600 cm−1, corresponding to the D band result from sp3 defects and G band on account of in-plane vibration of sp2 carbon of CDs, respectively. Two other characteristic peaks located at 566 and 690 cm−1, which are consistent with the recently research of NiFe LDH's related studies [20]. The UV–vis absorption spectra of SO3-CDs (Figure S14) typically describes an obvious optical absorption peak in 230 nm which is ascribed to π - π* transitions [46].

We further compared the functional groups of the P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH with SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH, NiFe LDH and SO3-CDs. Fourier transform-infrared (FT-IR) spectra were displayed in Fig. 4d. The high intensity peaks of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH at 1091 and 1159 cm−1 are corresponding to symmetric O = S = O and asymmetric O = S = O stretching modes [47–48] which indicated that the negatively charged functional groups (sulfonate functional groups) are intense aggregation on the surface with the assistance of the electric field. The curve also shows the stretching vibration peaks of O–H bond at 3400 cm−1 and C = O at 1635 cm−1. Furthermore, for other un-polarized samples, the peaks intensity of the sulfonate functional groups is relatively lower than that of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH. Hence, sulfonate functional groups are selectively exposed on the outside of the electrode, which will effectively facilitate OER processes [36].
In order to gain an insight understanding into excellent catalytic performance, DFT calculations have been conducted to investigate the mechanism of OER on P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH catalysts.

Figures S15-S18 show the intermediate structures of OER on P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH and NiFe LDH, respectively. We expose (010) surface of NiFe LDH, uncovering the Fe or Ni atom, the active site of the reaction, where the reaction occurs. In the OER process there are three intermediate states *O, *OH, and *OOH. Similarly, there are also three intermediate states in the OER process after sulfonate CDs were added near the Fe atoms of NiFe LDH. DFT calculations give each step’s free energy and the reaction coordinate on OER is shown in Fig. 5 a.

DFT calculations show that the addition of sulfonated CDs can effectively reduce the reaction energy barrier. Fig. 5a shows the reaction coordinate of OER on P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH and NiFe LDH. The rate-determining step of OER of both systems appear in *OOH to *O2, and its Gibbs free energy on P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH (ΔG = 2.08 eV) is lower than that of NiFe LDH (ΔG = 2.18 eV). This means that the introduction of sulfonated CDs reduces the energy barrier required for OER and facilitates the process. In OER reactions, the active site Fe first adsorbs the OH produced by water decomposition, then *OH loses its proton to produce *O, *O continues to combine with water to produce *OOH, and finally loses its proton to produce oxygen. On the P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH structure, the sulfonated groups on the surface of the sulfonated CDs can capture the proton and thus reduce the energy barrier of the steps of losing a proton. It is evident that the introduction of sulfonated CDs significantly reduces the reaction barrier from *OH to *O and *OOH to *O2.

Based on the above results and discussions, the proposed mechanism of the best OER performance of P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH nanocomposites is shown in Fig. 5b. The high catalytic activity was mainly attributable to NiFe LDH, while the addition of SO3-CDs undoubtedly further enhanced OER activity. First, SO3-CDs have the characteristics of small size, high conductivity and abundant functional groups on the surface. More importantly, the introduction of the electric field polarization selectively exposes the sulfonate functional groups on the SO3-CDs surface to obtain high OER activity via capturing proton by sulfonate functional groups, which plays an important role in reducing OER reaction barrier from *OH to *O and *OOH to *O2 to increase the catalytic performance [34]. Moreover, the anchoring between SO3-CDs and NiFe LDH was enhanced due to the polarization, leading to long-term electrochemical stability.

To sum up, we have demonstrated that electric field polarization can be employed for interface regulation of SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH nanocomposites to promote OER catalytic activity. P-SO3-CDs/NiFe LDH exhibits an excellent OER performance in 1 M KOH with a low overpotential of 200 mV at the current density of 10 mA cm−2 and a small a Tafel slope of 55.2 mV dec-1. When the catalysts were polarized, sulfonate functional groups selectively enrich at the surface of catalysts. Such sulfonate functional groups have a strong ability to capture proton and significantly decrease OER overpotential as confirmed by DFT calculation. Furthermore, the polarization increases the interaction of the composite catalysts and thus enhances their stability. This interfacial adjustment can selectively expose specific charged functional groups on the surface of catalysts and may be served as the purpose of selective catalysis. Such a convenient method may also be used in the interface design and regulation of other catalysts.

Wenxiang Zhu: Data curation, Visualization. Siyu Chen: Formal analysis. Fan Liao: Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Xiaodong Zhao: Software. Huixian Shi: Investigation. Yandi Shi: Software. Lai Xu: Calculation, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Qi Shao: Writing - review & editing. Zhenhui Kang: Project administration, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing, Supervision. Mingwang Shao: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by National MCF Energy R&D Program (2018YFE0306105), National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFA0204800, 2020YFA0406104, 2020YFA0406103, 2020YFA0406101), Innovative Research Group Project of the National Natural Science Foundation of China (51821002), National Natural Science Foundation of China (51902217, 91961120, 51725204, 21771132, 51972216, 52041202‬), Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190041), Collaborative Innovation Center of Suzhou Nano Science & Technology, the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions (PAPD), and the 111 Project and Joint International Research Laboratory of Carbon-Based Functional Materials and Devices .

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2021.129690.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
